refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall achieve and maintain operational availability at or above the customer-defined target threshold over the agreed evaluation period.
refined_fr.rationale: The original FR conflates two distinct obligations: (1) meeting a performance target and (2) sustaining it over time. The refined version separates these concerns while remaining solution-neutral. It avoids prescribing how availability is achieved (e.g., redundancy, predictive maintenance, rapid repair) and focuses on the measurable outcome in the problem domain. The phrase 'achieve and maintain' captures both the initial attainment and sustained performance without introducing implementation details.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Collect operational telemetry (uptime/downtime logs, scheduled vs. unscheduled maintenance events) over the agreed evaluation period. Calculate availability as (Total Time − Downtime) / Total Time. Compare against customer-defined target (e.g., ≥0.97). Verify that the measured availability meets or exceeds the threshold for the full evaluation window.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-004.1: The NGHE solution shall achieve the customer-defined operational availability target at the start of the evaluation period.
FR-NGHE-004.2: The NGHE solution shall sustain operational availability at or above the customer-defined target throughout the agreed evaluation period.

candidate_dps: DP1: Implement redundant critical subsystems (power, control, sensing) with automatic failover logic to mask single-point failures and maintain service continuity.
DP2: Design modular architecture with hot-swappable components and standardized interfaces to enable rapid replacement of failed units without system shutdown.
DP3: Establish predictive maintenance framework using condition monitoring and degradation models to detect and address faults before they cause unavailability.
DP4: Implement graceful degradation strategy where non-critical functions are suspended to preserve core operational capability when faults occur.
DP5: Deploy distributed control with local autonomy and inter-node synchronization to eliminate central points of failure and enable partial operation.
DP6: Establish comprehensive fault detection and isolation (FDIA) system with automated diagnostics and operator alerting to minimize mean-time-to-repair (MTTR).
DP7: Combine redundancy (DP1), modularity (DP2), and monitoring (DP3) in an integrated availability architecture with defined recovery time objectives (RTO) and recovery point objectives (RPO).
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP7: Integrated availability architecture combining (1) redundancy of critical subsystems with automatic failover, (2) modular design with hot-swappable components and standardized interfaces, and (3) predictive condition monitoring with automated fault detection and isolation—all governed by explicit RTO/RPO targets aligned to the 0.97 availability threshold.

potential_sources: Redundancy (DP1) and modularity (DP2) are orthogonal: redundancy addresses fault masking; modularity addresses repair speed. Both are necessary and reinforce each other.
Monitoring (DP3) and FDIA (DP6) are tightly coupled but intentionally so: condition data feeds diagnostics. This is a logical dependency, not an architectural defect.
RTO/RPO targets create a constraint on all three pillars: they must be jointly tuned. This is a coordination requirement, not a coupling failure.
Risk: If redundancy is oversized relative to MTTR improvements, availability gains plateau. Requires trade-study between redundancy cost and monitoring/repair effectiveness.
coupling_check.notes: DP7 avoids false independence by explicitly acknowledging that availability is a system property requiring coordinated solutions. No single DP alone achieves 0.97; the combination does. This is appropriate coupling at the architectural level.

assumptions_risks: ASSUMPTION: Redundant subsystems can be synchronized or reconciled without introducing new failure modes (e.g., split-brain scenarios in distributed control).
ASSUMPTION: Modular interfaces are stable and do not introduce latency or reliability penalties that offset modularity benefits.
ASSUMPTION: Condition monitoring data is sufficiently accurate and timely to enable predictive action before failures cascade.
ASSUMPTION: Worksite safety plan and operational policies permit automated failover and graceful degradation without manual intervention delays.
RISK: If MTTR is dominated by logistics (parts availability, technician travel) rather than technical diagnosis, monitoring improvements yield diminishing returns.
RISK: Redundancy adds complexity and potential for common-cause failures (e.g., shared power supply, environmental stress). Requires rigorous FMEA.
RISK: Evaluation period length and workload profile must be representative; short or atypical periods may not expose latent faults.
RISK: Customer-defined target may be aspirational without corresponding budget for redundancy and monitoring infrastructure.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine FR to explicitly state: 'The NGHE solution shall achieve and maintain operational availability ≥ [target] over [evaluation period] under [defined workload/duty cycle], with maximum tolerable downtime per incident of [RTO] and maximum acceptable data loss of [RPO]. Availability shall be measured as [uptime / (uptime + downtime)] excluding planned maintenance windows.' This clarifies the measurement basis and permits DP7 to be decomposed into sub-DPs for redundancy architecture, modularity standards, and monitoring thresholds. Additionally, conduct a trade-study matrix: (redundancy cost vs. MTTR reduction vs. availability gain) to validate that DP7 is economically justified relative to simpler alternatives (e.g., DP3 alone with very aggressive maintenance).

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: System-level availability architecture encompassing redundancy coordination, failover orchestration, modular component lifecycle management, condition monitoring integration, and RTO/RPO governance across all subsystems

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Ingests health and performance metrics from Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems to feed predictive condition monitoring and fault detection logic

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Receives operational directives and reports availability state; coordinates failover decisions that affect task execution routing

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Emits critical fault isolation notifications and failover events that trigger safety-critical responses

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Manages redundant communication paths and failover signaling between primary and backup subsystem instances

module_containment_scores.notes: Highest containment: NGHE Solution is the system-level orchestration layer responsible for cross-subsystem redundancy, failover logic, RTO/RPO governance, and condition monitoring coordination. Redundancy architecture, failover state machines, and availability targets are naturally owned here. Minor diffusion: Vehicle and Teleoperation must implement modular, hot-swappable design patterns and expose health telemetry, but the orchestration logic resides in NGHE Solution.

Poor containment: Vehicle is a subsystem, not a system-level orchestrator. Redundancy coordination, failover logic, and RTO/RPO governance span multiple subsystems and cannot be localized to Vehicle alone. Condition monitoring and fault isolation would diffuse across Teleoperation and Safety Services. Vehicle can implement modular, hot-swappable components but cannot own the integrated availability architecture.

Poor containment: Teleoperation is a subsystem focused on remote operation, not system-level availability. Redundancy, failover orchestration, and RTO/RPO targets require visibility and control across Vehicle, Safety Services, and network infrastructure. Condition monitoring would diffuse across multiple domains. Teleoperation can participate in modular design but cannot own the integrated architecture.

Weak containment: Safety Services handles safety-critical responses but does not own system-level availability orchestration. Redundancy coordination, failover logic, and RTO/RPO governance are broader than safety concerns alone. Predictive monitoring and fault isolation span operational and safety domains. Safety Services receives SafetyAlert outputs but does not orchestrate the integrated availability architecture.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: DP7 (integrated availability architecture) is fundamentally a system-level design pattern that coordinates redundancy, failover, monitoring, and availability targets across all subsystems. NGHE Solution, as the top-level system orchestrator, is the natural owner. Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services are consumers and implementers of modular, hot-swappable design patterns and health telemetry providers, but they do not own the orchestration logic or RTO/RPO governance.
containment_risks: Risk: If NGHE Solution becomes a monolithic orchestrator without clear internal decomposition, failover logic and condition monitoring may become tightly coupled, reducing maintainability.

Risk: Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems must implement standardized interfaces and modular design; if these subsystems resist modularity, the availability architecture will diffuse into ad-hoc failover patches.
Risk: Predictive condition monitoring requires deep integration with subsystem telemetry; if telemetry schemas are inconsistent or incomplete, fault detection accuracy will degrade.
Risk: RTO/RPO targets are system-level SLAs; if subsystem dependencies are not explicitly mapped, failover orchestration may not meet availability thresholds under cascading failures.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: DP7 is well-aligned with NGHE Solution ownership. However, to reduce orchestration complexity and improve containment, consider decomposing DP7 into three tightly-scoped sub-DPs: (1) DP7a: Redundancy and failover orchestration (owned by NGHE Solution), (2) DP7b: Modular component design with standardized interfaces (owned by Vehicle and Teleoperation, with NGHE Solution defining interface contracts), (3) DP7c: Predictive condition monitoring and fault isolation (owned by a dedicated monitoring subsystem or NGHE Solution with pluggable analytics). This decomposition clarifies ownership boundaries and reduces diffusion of monitoring logic across subsystems.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP7 (integrated availability architecture) directly addresses FR requirement for operational availability ≥0.97. The DP explicitly maps three complementary mechanisms (redundancy with failover, modular hot-swappable design, predictive monitoring) to the functional requirement and anchors them to RTO/RPO targets aligned to the 0.97 threshold. The logic chain is sound: redundancy + failover + monitoring + explicit SLA governance = sustained availability. No logical gaps detected.

diffusion_risks: CRITICAL: Predictive condition monitoring logic is underspecified in IM allocation. The DP states 'automated fault detection and isolation' but does not clarify whether this resides in NGHE Solution, Vehicle, Teleoperation, or a dedicated monitoring subsystem. Current containment analysis shows 0.65–0.70 diffusion scores for Vehicle and Teleoperation, indicating monitoring responsibilities may scatter across subsystems without clear ownership.

HIGH: Modular design and hot-swappable component standards are stated as DP elements but lack explicit IM ownership. Vehicle and Teleoperation must implement these patterns, but no module boundary defines who owns the interface contract specification, validation, and enforcement. Risk of ad-hoc modularity if subsystems interpret 'standardized interfaces' differently.

HIGH: Failover orchestration logic is assigned to NGHE Solution (0.92 containment), but the DP does not specify how failover state machines interact with Tasking (inout interface) and SafetyAlert (out interface). If failover decisions trigger safety-critical responses without explicit handoff protocols, coordination logic may diffuse into Safety Services.

MEDIUM: RTO/RPO governance is stated as 'governed by explicit RTO/RPO targets' but lacks IM-level detail on who owns target definition, monitoring, and enforcement. If subsystems independently interpret RTO/RPO thresholds, failover timing may become inconsistent.

MEDIUM: Telemetry ingestion (in interface) feeds condition monitoring, but no IM specifies telemetry schema standardization, validation, or versioning. Inconsistent telemetry formats across Vehicle and Teleoperation will degrade fault detection accuracy and diffuse monitoring logic into data transformation layers.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution is correctly identified as the primary owner (0.92 containment) for redundancy coordination, failover orchestration, and RTO/RPO governance. However, DP7 conflates three distinct concerns: (1) orchestration logic (high containment in NGHE Solution), (2) component modularity standards (distributed ownership across Vehicle/Teleoperation with NGHE Solution defining contracts), and (3) condition monitoring (currently underspecified, high diffusion risk). The recommended decomposition into DP7a/DP7b/DP7c is sound and necessary to reduce diffusion. Current IM boundary is too coarse; NGHE Solution must be internally decomposed into failover orchestration, monitoring coordination, and interface contract management sub-modules to prevent monolithic coupling.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: PARTIAL. Acceptance criterion 'Measured availability ≥0.97 over agreed evaluation period' is measurable and traceable to NGHE Solution's RTO/RPO governance. However, verification is blocked by unclear IM allocation for condition monitoring and telemetry standards. Without explicit ownership of fault detection logic and telemetry schema, test plans cannot isolate which module is responsible for availability failures. Recommend: (1) Define telemetry schema and validation rules as owned by a dedicated module or NGHE Solution's monitoring sub-component, (2) Specify fault detection algorithms and their IM ownership before testing, (3) Create explicit RTO/RPO measurement protocols tied to each subsystem's failover responsibilities.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: LOW. DP7 is allocated to NGHE Solution as a system-level architecture, but allocation is incomplete: (1) Modular design standards are stated but not allocated to a specific module responsible for interface contract definition and enforcement, (2) Predictive monitoring is mentioned but not allocated—unclear if owned by NGHE Solution, Vehicle, Teleoperation, or a separate monitoring service, (3) Failover orchestration is allocated to NGHE Solution but lacks sub-module clarity on state machine ownership, failover decision logic, and coordination with Tasking/SafetyAlert interfaces, (4) RTO/RPO governance is allocated to NGHE Solution but lacks detail on who measures, reports, and enforces targets. Recommend: Decompose DP7 into three sub-DPs with explicit IM ownership: DP7a (Failover Orchestration → NGHE Solution Failover Coordinator), DP7b (Modular Interface Standards → NGHE Solution Architecture Board + Vehicle/Teleoperation implementers), DP7c (Condition Monitoring → NGHE Solution Monitoring Engine or dedicated subsystem).

recommended_revisions: DECOMPOSE DP7 into three tightly-scoped sub-DPs with explicit IM ownership: (1) DP7a: Failover orchestration with explicit state machines, RTO/RPO enforcement, and Tasking/SafetyAlert coordination (owned by NGHE Solution Failover Coordinator module), (2) DP7b: Modular component design with standardized interface contracts, hot-swap protocols, and validation rules (owned by NGHE Solution Architecture Board; implemented by Vehicle and Teleoperation), (3) DP7c: Predictive condition monitoring with standardized telemetry schema, fault detection algorithms, and automated isolation logic (owned by NGHE Solution Monitoring Engine or dedicated monitoring subsystem).

DEFINE telemetry schema and validation rules as a formal artifact owned by NGHE Solution (or dedicated monitoring module). Specify: (1) Required health metrics from Vehicle and Teleoperation, (2) Telemetry format, versioning, and backward compatibility, (3) Validation rules and error handling for malformed or missing telemetry, (4) Latency and frequency requirements for condition monitoring accuracy.

SPECIFY failover orchestration logic with explicit state machines, decision trees, and coordination protocols. Document: (1) Failover trigger conditions (RTO/RPO thresholds, fault detection signals), (2) Failover decision logic and subsystem dependencies, (3) Handoff protocols with Tasking (task rerouting) and SafetyAlert (safety-critical notifications), (4) Rollback and recovery procedures.

ALLOCATE RTO/RPO governance to a dedicated sub-module within NGHE Solution (e.g., SLA Manager). Define: (1) RTO/RPO target definition and customer alignment, (2) Measurement methodology and reporting cadence, (3) Enforcement mechanisms (alerts, escalation, failover triggers), (4) Subsystem-level RTO/RPO dependencies and critical path analysis.

CREATE interface contract specifications for modular design and hot-swappable components. Define: (1) Standardized interface definitions (APIs, protocols, data formats), (2) Modularity requirements for Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems, (3) Validation and certification procedures for new components, (4) Ownership and governance of interface evolution.

MAP subsystem dependencies and failure modes to RTO/RPO targets. Conduct: (1) Dependency analysis (which subsystems must be available for overall system availability), (2) Failure mode analysis (cascading failures, single points of failure), (3) Redundancy strategy validation (does redundancy placement meet RTO/RPO targets?), (4) Stress testing under degraded conditions (partial subsystem failures, network latency).

CLARIFY ownership of condition monitoring analytics. Decide: (1) Is predictive monitoring owned by NGHE Solution or a dedicated monitoring subsystem? (2) Who owns fault detection algorithms and their tuning? (3) How are monitoring results fed back to failover orchestration? (4) What is the latency requirement for fault detection to failover decision?

ADD explicit acceptance criteria for IM-level verification: (1) Telemetry schema compliance across Vehicle and Teleoperation (100% of required metrics present and valid), (2) Failover orchestration latency (failover decision to subsystem switchover ≤ X seconds), (3) Fault detection accuracy (false positive rate ≤ Y%, detection latency ≤ Z seconds), (4) RTO/RPO target achievement (measured availability ≥0.97 over evaluation period).

decision.justification: FR/DP gate passes (logical soundness confirmed), but DP/IM and FR/IM gates fail critically. The DP7 design is conceptually correct but architecturally underspecified. Three distinct concerns (failover orchestration, modular interface standards, condition monitoring) are conflated into a single coarse DP with diffusion scores of 0.65–0.92 across subsystems. The DP/IM gate identifies CRITICAL diffusion risks in predictive monitoring ownership, HIGH risks in failover coordination with safety-critical interfaces (Tasking, SafetyAlert), and HIGH risks in modular design standards enforcement. Verification is blocked by unclear IM allocation. Given high safety criticality, unresolved coordination between failover logic and SafetyAlert interface poses unacceptable risk. Decomposition into three tightly-scoped sub-FRs with explicit IM ownership is mandatory before proceeding to detailed design. Stopping here would leave architectural ambiguity that will propagate into implementation and testing phases.
next_level_sub_frs: FR7a: Orchestrate failover decisions and subsystem switchover to meet RTO/RPO targets while maintaining safety-critical interface coordination (Tasking rerouting, SafetyAlert notification).
FR7b: Enforce standardized modular component design and hot-swappable interface contracts across Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems to enable rapid component replacement without system redesign.
FR7c: Detect incipient faults and isolate failed components through automated condition monitoring using standardized telemetry, enabling predictive failover before availability degradation.

revise.why: DP7 must be decomposed into three sub-DPs with explicit IM ownership before next-level decomposition: (1) DP7a: Failover orchestration state machine with explicit RTO/RPO enforcement and safety-critical handoff protocols (owned by NGHE Solution Failover Coordinator module), (2) DP7b: Standardized interface contract framework with modularity validation rules (owned by NGHE Solution Architecture Board; implemented by Vehicle/Teleoperation), (3) DP7c: Condition monitoring engine with standardized telemetry schema and fault detection algorithms (owned by NGHE Solution Monitoring Engine or dedicated subsystem). Current DP7 is too coarse and conflates orchestration logic (high containment) with distributed modularity standards (medium containment) and underspecified monitoring (high diffusion). Decomposition reduces diffusion risk and clarifies IM boundaries before sub-FR derivation.

assumptions: Failover orchestration logic can be centralized in NGHE Solution without creating a single point of failure (redundancy of orchestrator itself is assumed to be addressed at DP7a level).
Telemetry ingestion from Vehicle and Teleoperation can be standardized through a schema contract without requiring major refactoring of existing subsystems.
SafetyAlert interface can accept asynchronous notifications from failover orchestration without introducing race conditions or safety-critical timing violations.
RTO/RPO targets are customer-defined and stable; decomposition assumes targets are known before IM allocation.
Modular design standards (hot-swappable components) are technically feasible for both Vehicle and Teleoperation subsystems without architectural conflicts.
open_risks: CRITICAL: Failover orchestration state machine may interact with SafetyAlert in ways that create safety-critical race conditions. Explicit coordination protocol (handoff timing, acknowledgment, rollback) must be defined at DP7a level before implementation. Recommend: Conduct FMEA on failover-to-SafetyAlert interaction; define state machine with explicit safety guards.

CRITICAL: Predictive condition monitoring ownership is unresolved. If monitoring logic diffuses across Vehicle, Teleoperation, and NGHE Solution, fault detection accuracy will degrade and failover decisions may be delayed. Recommend: Assign monitoring ownership to a single module (NGHE Solution Monitoring Engine preferred) with clear interface contracts for telemetry ingestion and fault signal output.

HIGH: Telemetry schema standardization is not yet defined. Without explicit schema ownership and versioning, Vehicle and Teleoperation may emit incompatible telemetry formats, breaking condition monitoring. Recommend: Define telemetry schema as a formal artifact owned by NGHE Solution; include validation rules, backward compatibility, and latency requirements.

HIGH: Modular design standards lack enforcement mechanism. If Vehicle and Teleoperation interpret 'standardized interfaces' differently, hot-swap capability will fail. Recommend: Create interface contract specifications with validation procedures; assign NGHE Solution Architecture Board as owner of contract evolution and enforcement.

HIGH: RTO/RPO governance is stated but not operationalized. Without explicit measurement methodology and enforcement mechanisms, subsystems may independently interpret targets, leading to inconsistent failover timing. Recommend: Define SLA Manager sub-module within NGHE Solution to own target definition, measurement, reporting, and enforcement.

MEDIUM: Failover orchestration latency is not specified. If failover decision-to-switchover time exceeds acceptable bounds, availability target may not be met. Recommend: Define explicit latency budget (e.g., ≤5 seconds) at DP7a level; validate against RTO/RPO targets.

MEDIUM: Cascading failure modes are not analyzed. If failover of one subsystem triggers failures in dependent subsystems, overall availability may degrade despite local redundancy. Recommend: Conduct dependency analysis and failure mode propagation study before finalizing DP7a/b/c.
evidence_needed: Failover orchestration state machine specification with explicit transitions, trigger conditions (RTO/RPO thresholds, fault signals), and safety-critical handoff protocols to Tasking and SafetyAlert interfaces.
Telemetry schema definition (required metrics, format, versioning, validation rules, latency/frequency requirements) owned by NGHE Solution or dedicated monitoring module.
Interface contract specifications for modular design and hot-swappable components, including standardized API/protocol definitions, modularity requirements, and validation procedures.
RTO/RPO target definition and measurement methodology, including subsystem-level dependencies, critical path analysis, and enforcement mechanisms (alerts, escalation, failover triggers).
Dependency analysis and failure mode propagation study showing how subsystem failures cascade and how redundancy placement mitigates cascading failures.
Failover latency budget and validation against RTO/RPO targets; stress testing results under degraded conditions (partial subsystem failures, network latency).
Condition monitoring accuracy metrics (fault detection latency, false positive rate, false negative rate) and validation against availability target.
Safety-critical interaction analysis between failover orchestration and SafetyAlert interface, including race condition assessment and rollback procedures.
Ownership and governance model for interface contract evolution (who approves changes, how are breaking changes managed, versioning strategy).
